name: Saudi Arabia

colors.symbolism: purity; the colour of the shahada inscription and the sword

description: A green field bearing in white the Arabic shahada (the Islamic declaration of faith) written in Thuluth script, with a horizontal white sword beneath it (point toward the hoist). Both obverse and reverse show the inscription reading correctly (right-to-left), which requires that the flag be made of two stitched layers.
history: The current design was adopted on 15 March 1973, refining earlier flags of the House of Saud that had borne the shahada and a sword from the early 20th century. The shahada-and-sword motif on a green field originates with the Wahhabi religious movement and the First Saudi State (1744). Because of the religious sanctity of the shahada, the flag is never lowered to half-mast, never reproduced on commercial merchandise, and is constructed with mirrored obverse and reverse so the inscription always reads correctly.

features.symbolism: Islam; the colour traditionally associated with the Prophet Muhammad and his banner
the shahada — 'There is no god but God; Muhammad is the Messenger of God' (لا إله إلا الله محمد رسول الله) — the central declaration of Islamic faith
the strict application of justice; commemorates the military conquests of King Abdulaziz Ibn Saud, founder of modern Saudi Arabia
